[IMAGE:0]It's almost midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will more than likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The diagram to the left?  The three primary transcendent functions in classic sacred geometry, the √2 (square root of 2) √3 (square root of 3), and φ (Golden Section) ratios, expressed geometrically by the square and its diagonal, the triangle and its altitude, and the pentagon and pentagram. The last obstacle to the reprinting of my Sacred Geometry Oracle is finally out of the way, the cards are rolling off the presses of a firm in Malta -- not at all inappropriate, as Malta has famous prehistoric temples with fascinating sacred geometry -- and I expect to be able sometime soon to announce a firm release date rather earlier than the publisher had expected. So some sacred geometry seems appropriate! 

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

comments.content:
Good Evening,

Congratulations on the Sacred Geometry oracle moving forward! Thanks as always for kindly answering so many questions and hosting this forum for others to answer as well.

1. Related to your announcement: if I have the previous sacred geometry oracle with the unfortunate 90s-ish patina'd industrial graphic design, will there be much different in the new printing besides an aesthetic more in-keeping with the material and your vision?
2. I'll be adding Spirit Below to my SOP practice tomorrow, and it seems a bit different from the gates of the 4 elements - besides "check in with the deity you're invoking that he/she is okay with it and follow the directions in the DMH," any advice?
3. What led you to write a series of books on a (mostly) non-magical occult path (you just-announced "The Way of the Golden Section")? Just filling an unmet need, or was there more to it than that?
4. My memory and search skills are failing me - in one or more of your posts/essays, you mentioned a book that discusses stories of magic done by Native Americans prior to the coming of Europeans that include accounts of feats outside the scope of what magic seems to be able to accomplish today (such as obvious and noticeable effects on physical objects) - was this God is Red or another title?
5. This might be hard to answer, but how much of your current understanding of the planes is from teachings/writings, and how much from your own meditation/perception?
6. For either JMG or the commentariat, what energy centers/meridians are associated with the muscles to either side of the spine where it meets the hips? And/or, what are some good resources to learn about the links between the energetic body and the physical body?

6. The Urinary Bladder meridians runs along the erector spinae muscles, and the gall bladder meridians run lateral to the UB meridians. The urinary bladder is related to the water element, the gall bladder to the wood element in Chinese medicine. Water in Chinese theory isn't the same as Water as you're encountering it in the SOP, though, and you can cause yourself serious trouble if you try to conflate the two.

In order to learn about the links between the subtle body and the physical body, you need to study either TCM or ayurveda. There are hints in the European tradition, which you ought to study as well, but that tradition is fragmentary. Eventually someone is going to use a working knowledge of the various Eastern systems to reverse engineer a complete Western energetic system, but we don't have that yet.

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry Oracle, Spirit Below, Golden Section, Pre-Columbian Magic, Planes, Energy Body
replies.content:
6) The Dai Mai (Girdling or Belt Meridian/Channel/Vessel) may be close to what you're talking about - it's one of the Eight Extraordinary Meridians or Vessels, connects to Du-4 at the spine, and includes points along the Gall Bladder and Liver Vessels. Extraordinary Vessels don't have their own points, they connect other pathways. Maciocia's Foundations of Chinese Medicine has good chapters on the Extraordinary Vessels and the Girdling Vessel. Damo Mitchell also has written very clear material on the energetic body from a Daoist internal alchemy perspective, and his free class on the Microcosmic Orbit offers useful theoretical material.
Be aware when looking at the Chinese models that the Organs aren't exactly organs in the Western sense, and the Five Xing (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, Water) are not Elements at all, they have nothing whatsoever to do with the Elements; confusion with them was the result of a mistranslation. They are rather phases of transformation of things, processes of change, and changing interrelationships of things. Their purpose is not to describe static essence or substance. They have a closer correspondence to planetary energies.
The subtle body models from Asia (TCM and Daoist alchemical, Tibetan Sowa Rigpa and Tantric, and Indian Ayurvedic and Tantric) are good places to start, but I think the best and safest practical way is to learn from the beginning to relax deeply and begin passively sensing the etheric body and subtle pathways for yourself; begin developing awareness of it while opening, releasing, and letting go of tension, without trying to move it or manipulate it. The charts and so on are nice to know about, but they make a lot more sense when you can really perceive what the charts are trying to capture.
Even simple relaxation, meditation, and the feeling of creating space in the body triggers movements to occur on their own, and you can follow their pathways and begin sensing blockages and so on. Relaxing against a tree (a simple form of Tree Qigong) can cause quite noticeable effects. Notice the effects of states of emotional and sexual arousal. You can begin distinguishing different qualities (electric, magnetic, hot, cold, heavy, light, a feeling like rushing water). Once you are aware of your energetic baseline, then you can begin sensing and mapping out the effects of Western magical and esoteric practices have on your etheric body. It seems that a great deal isn't yet known. At the physiological level there is a relationship to the nervous and endocrine systems. Franz Bardon's system, classical homeopathy, and humoral medicine have useful theoretical things to add to this kind of investigation.
Once enough people have done this sort of mapping out for themselves, maybe we can then begin to compare notes!

I was a child of the early 50s, so I recall that period very well. There was a definite sense that something unpleasant had been unleashed, although at the time it was difficult to believe that it could be positively evil. The ethos of the sixties, for all its occasional silliness, was never vindictive or evil. When Thatcher came to power in 1979, people were therefore slow to catch on, because they simply had no experience of governments that actually wanted to do evil, and damage the social and economic infrastructure of the country. Thatcher not only caused, she publicly gloried in, massive increases in unemployment and poverty and the destruction of much of the social fabric of the country, as well as selling off anything that wasn't nailed down to her cronies. Now I'd lived through governments I disapproved of, but there were limits. This was, I think, the first time anyone in the Uk had actually encountered a government that seemed deliberately bent on doing evil, almost like a pantomime villain.

Reading your work and that of others, it's tempting to wonder if there were not demonic forces at work then, perhaps summoned into being as a reaction to the tolerant and generally optimistic mood I recall from the 60s. Certainly, Thatcher was incredibly lucky: she became Tory Party leader by accident, when those using her as a stalking horse got their sums wrong. She won an election in 1979 that she would have lost if it had been a few months earlier or later. She was almost brought down by the Falklands crisis, but saved by the skill and bravery of the military. She survived an assassination attempt by the IRA because she was in the next room when the bomb went off. She stayed in power in 1983 only because the Opposition decided to split into two parties and fight each other. When people said she had "the luck of the devil" at the time I thought it was just a figure of speech. But caricatures of her as a witch were extremely common, and the folk-image that remains is of someone not quite real, not quite human, with staring eyes, prominent teeth and a a screeching voice, a sort of nightmare governess. It's not surprising that, in the week she died, the song that shot to the top of the charts in Britain was "Ding Dong the Witch is Dead."

I do think there was something strange going on, and that Thatcher, in the end, was probably more a vehicle for the destruction of traditions and societies than a knowing participant. Her image was almost entirely manufactured by specialist PR experts, from the fake upper-class accent and the almost psychopathic cultivated lack of empathy, to the careful photographs, always taken from below (in person she was a short, dumpy, unimpressive woman) to the presentation as some kind of semi-divine saviour of the country - the "iron lady" (in fact she was notoriously indecisive). The Queen of the Night, perhaps?

replies.content:
There are several factors going on here. First of all, clairvoyance is unusually common among people of Celtic ancestry, and so Evans-Wentz's report isn't necessarily a guide to how the world in general was in 1909. That said, we have quite a few sources of data suggesting that human interaction with nonphysical realities is much more difficult now than it was at various points in the past. It's been a long, slow, ragged process of decline.

There are at least two large-scale transformations that seem to be linked to this process. The first is that civilizations, as they age, sink deeper into the material plane. Nonphysical experiences of all kinds tend to be very common in dark ages, fade out gradually as things settle down, become rare enough that an Age of Reason becomes plausible...and then when that collapses and decline gets under way, they start to pick up again.

The second is the precessional cycle. If I read it correctly, spiritual experience is easiest during the ages of Leo and Cancer, ruled by the Sun and Moon respectively, and hardest during the ages of Aquarius and Capricorn, traditionally ruled by old bleak Saturn. If that's the case we're going to have to get used to spiritual experience being something very quiet and subtle for a while, because we've got 4187 years to go before the dawn of the Sagittarian age and the beginning of the upward arc.

replies.title: McCarthy's Book is Excellent
replies.content:
Josephine McCarthy's book is an excellent place to start IMO.

I think it is safe to say the world could use a few more exorcists.

Her book is the only one written from a Western Mystery Tradition perspective that I am aware of. It also does not get caught up in the "glamour of the exorcist" that gets flaunted about in the Hollywood version of this role. It also does not work under the same assumptions as say an exorcist in the Catholic church would be working.

Part of the problem with finding good literature in this niche area is that a lot of the books are written from a fundamentalist Christian perspective, and no disrespect to practicing Christians, but parasites, demons, and other beings that cause trouble in the human world -or humans who end up dabbling and bring trouble on themselves, and then need a mage to sort them out-; these beings don't necessarily care about what religion a person is or isn't. I don't deny that Latin words and prayers may have power, especially if a person is consecrated, and has done other spiritual and magical work, but as McCarthy has shown there is a lot more to exorcism than browbeating a being in Latin. The work is very nuanced.

A lot of the work also seems to be about unbinding, undoing curses, etc. The nastier denizens of the astral and beyond seem to end up getting called in when a curse is involved. Beyond that working with Faery/Land beings whom humans may have unwittingly disturbed is another issue -but these aren't demons, yet still the tool kit of "exorcism" can help as the Mage retrains the people involved how to get along.

If you want to get a taste of it before buying the book, you can listen to her being interviewed about this book by Greg Kaminsky of the excellent Occult of Personality podcast:

https://occultofpersonality.net/podcast-100-josephine-mccarthy/

Everything else out there that I've seen on the subject seems to be either Catholic, fundamentalist Evangelical, or "ooh look at me, I'm so dark, I'm an exorcist" kind of stuff.

McCarthy's book opens up the path beyond all that for others who feel called to follow.

comments.title: Wendigos
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This is really just a musing more than a question, but it occurred to me the other day that the modern 'zombie' phenomenon is not actually about true, Haitian zombies at all - it's just a co-opting of the name (as you note in your book Monsters - looking forward to the revised copy in a few weeks, by the way!). The far more closely related supernatural creature, to me at least, seems to actually be the insatiably hungry, cannibalistic, half-rotting Wendigo. (Maybe zombie is just a catchier name?)

The whole modern zombie phenomenon seemed to get started some time around 2005-2008ish, then really got into general popular culture in the early 2010s. This seems significant, as this was immediately following peak oil, and then the housing crash of 2008. Factoring in your recent post on the other blog about negative sum economies, it makes a lot more sense - in a negative sum economy, people must metaphorically eat one another and take their strength (or material wealth) to survive. The greater the desperation, the greater the hunger, and all the wealth in the world will never be 'enough'. They are also supposed to have a heart of ice, the symbolism of which is obvious.

Combine this with corona and woke culture, both of which provide ample ways to 'outgroup' others, stripping them of their status and humanity, whilst at least theoretically increasing one's own status, and thereby metaphorically 'consuming' them, and you have a dangerous combination.

Although medically discredited nowadays, 'wendigo psychosis' was at one point a recognised and attested condition where, after consuming human flesh out of necessity, an individual would get a taste for it and forever crave more, resorting to a semi-feral, pariah status.
Amongst the native American tribes, an excessively greedy person could quite easily become a wendigo as well, and the only cure was either a special ritual to shock them out of it (typically involving a lot of vomiting) or, in the worst cases, death.

The use of the word psychosis in the medical term is an interesting one as well, as you've previously said that the only thing that will shake the western world out of its collective mass psychosis over corona is (in the words of Gilbert and Sullivan), a short sharp shock, or... the more terminal option.

Mr. Crow

Good morning. What is the occult view of depression, and what is involved in its resolution? Perhaps you know of some historical case studies.

Thank you,
Dylan

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
What I was taught was that depression is what you get when you repress anger.

It's a little more complex than that, because anger is a secondary emotion -- that is, it's always got some other emotion underneath it. The primary emotion is usually fear, shame, or grief. These are difficult emotions to bear, so it's quite common for people to make themselves feel angry instead, because anger is energizing and so easier to put up with than fear, shame, or grief.

But children very often are not permitted to express anger toward their parents without facing savage emotional or physical abuse. So very often children who've hidden their fear, shame, or grief under a layer of anger, then repress the anger. Depression is the result.

So resolving depression involves, first of all, getting through the repression to experience the anger that's beneath it, and then getting through the anger to experience and accept the primary emotion underneath that. It's a long, complicated process.

replies.content:
Culianu talks about something similar in his book (
Culianu, Ioan P.
Eros and magic in the Renaissance.
):


(iv) Ejaculation and Retention of Semen
Some passages of De vinculis are especially interesting because they seem to show that the practice of coitus reservatus was not foreign to Bruno's magic. We know that this was practiced by Taoists in ChinaS and the tantric yogis in India and Tibet.9
Bruno's remarks are so concise, however, that great care is needed to define their meaning without misrepresentation. Since only a few sen- tences are involved, we can make an exception to the general rule ob- served in this book so that readers can consult the Latin text as well as the translation:


Iactu seminis vincula relaxantur, retentione vero intenduntur; tal- iter debet affectus qui vincire vult, qualiter qui vincire debet. Prop- terea in conviviis et post convivia inspirare introducitur in ossibus ignem Cupido. Vide. Continentia est principium vinculi, abstinen-
tia praecurrit famem, haec melius cibum attrahit.
 (De vinc., p. 645: Ejaculation of semen releases the bonds, whereas its reten- tion strengthens them. He who wishes to enchain is obliged to develop the same emotions as he who must be bound. That is why, when we are overheated at banquets or after banquets, Cupid invades us. Look: continence is the begin- ning of the bondage, abstinence precedes hunger, and hun- ger leads to victuals.)


Vinculum fit ex prolifico semine quod ad actum suum rapitur, nititur atque rapit; ideo hos emissum secundum partem, perit secun- dum partem vinculi vis. (Ibid., p. 663: There is a bond by means of prolific semen, which is attracted, strives, and approaches its act. That is why, if it has been partially emitted, the strength of the bond is also partially dissipated.)
Cupidinis vincula, quae ante coitum intensa erant, modico seminis iactu sunt remissa et ignes temperati, obiecto pulchro nihilominus eodem permanente. (Ibid.: Cupid's bonds, which were strong before the mating, were dissipated after the moderate ejacu- lation of semen, and the ardor was diminished even though the attractive object did not cease to be.)
Let us agree that Bruno's notes, concise to the point of unintelligibil- ity, can give rise to several interpretations. We have already stated a first hypothesis: that he deals with the practice of retention of sperm, of coitus reservatus. We know that by means of such a practice, along with exercises of "embryonic breathing," the Taoists sought vitality and lon- gevity, whereas the Tantrics, within the framework of a subtle and much more sophisticated physiology, were supposed, through the mai- thuna, to reawaken dormant cosmic energies and to channel them to the "Lotus of a thousand leaves" at the top of the head, entering a state of ecstasy. In both cases, coitus reservatus represents one of the indispens- able methods to reach the goal.
Since, in a treatise on erotic magic, Bruno speaks of the retention of sperm, we may ask whether he does not have in mind a practice of the same kind.
We soon discover he is not thinking of that. What interests him, as we know, is the way we can seduce, create bonds and attachments. Now, he observes that once pleasure has been had, the bonds dissolve. That is why, to maintain the strength of a bond, it must not be enjoyed.

replies.title: Re: Help with birds
replies.content:
I work with birds, but generally more often in terms of their flight patterns.

I can’t say as I’m expert in your specific scenarios, but the common factor that stood out to me was the birds not seeing you (your house, your car), or not perceiving you as a risk to their health and well-being.

Three possible reasons come immediately to mind. There may be more - my advice would be to ask a competent diviner for help in understanding the situation.

1. It’s completely coincidence

2. Sometimes not being perceived can happen as a result of being out of right relationship with the world because of curse energy.

3. There could be something incoming that you’re not perceiving as a genuine risk, which you might be being cautioned to plan for.

As I said, though, the bet would be to ask a competent diviner to read for you, to get some specific clarity.

Good luck!

replies.title: Re: Help with birds
replies.content:
This is my interpretation, based on not much (less than three years so far of self-study based mostly on observation and intuition). With the present sorry state of the art in augury, we have to look for the most straightforward, nearly literal, messages because that's all we have any chance of reading.

Bird collisions with clear glass windows (something the ancient Roman augurs didn't experience) are very common, too common to be strongly bad omens. (I had to put paracord "bars" outside my largest window to protect the birds from frequent strikes.) But when you're behind the glass at the time it's a connection; the oracle influencing the birds is literally making contact, with some urgency. A bird impeding your travel means reconsider a path you're on, expressing some trust that with second thoughts you will make a better choice. (If you or your husband had struck the bird, that would have been a bad omen.) Considering where your husband and you were on the way to when (2) and (4) happened might clarify.

The experience of having someone suddenly become happier and nicer is a fairly common one, but it has some interesting lessons to teach. Our relationships with other people involve subtle connections of which we aren't conscious: we sense their attitude, they sense ours, and that shapes the behavior of both people. Once you work with the Law of Acceptance and process your memories of somebody, that changes how you relate to them, and how they relate to you changes accordingly. Yes, it's magic -- a gentle healing magic, but magic nonetheless.

And your bodies are becoming more alive. As you deal with old emotions, the energy locked up in them flows freely again, and that influences your physical as well as your etheric bodies.

In the FAQ you have:

Which of your magic books are compatible with one another?

The books of magical instruction I’ve written so far fall into three groups:

Classic Golden Dawn Group: Learning Ritual Magic, Paths of Wisdom, Circles of Power

Druidry Group: The Druidry Handbook, The Druid Magic Handbook, The Druid Grove Handbook, The Gnostic Celtic Church, The Dolmen Arch (forthcoming)

Celtic Golden Dawn Group: The Celtic Golden Dawn, The Coelbren Alphabet, Merlin’s Wheel (forthcoming), The Celtic Cabala (forthcoming)

replies.title: Re: Astrology Possibilities
replies.content:
1) Astrology goes extraordinarily deep. It's a symbolic, philosophical, and divinatory system as rich and extensive as the Cabala, and if there are limits to what you can do with it, I haven't yet caught sight of them.

2) Glad to hear you like the cookbook!

3) I write about tools, not about my personal experiences, which I figure aren't of interest to anybody but me. (Most autobiographies are stunningly dull.)
